data.title: RBA on Track to Raise Rates, but What's the Rush? — Market Talk
data.description: While there's a case for the Reserve Bank of Australia to raise interest rates in March based on Wednesday's strong 4Q25 GDP data, the numbers don't clearly point to a need for a back-to-back boost, says Jonathan Kearns, chief economist at Challenger. Private demand was soft in the quarter and productivity growth remained at 1% helping to bring unit labor costs growth down to a respectable 3.2%, the slowest rate since before the pandemic, Kearns, a former senior manager at the RBA, said. Given the RBA's data-dependent approach, the central bank will wait for the quarterly CPI and increase in May, he says. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

data.title: Broadcom's 1Q Impresses Investors Despite High Valuation — Market Talk
data.description: Broadcom shares rise 5% after earnings, despite skepticism among investors. Investors' expectations were especially lofty because of Broadcom's high stock valuation, says Jake Behan, head of capital markets at Direxion, making it a formidable competitor to Nvidia. Behan says Broadcom's $10 billion share buyback authorization is helping the stock after hours. Broadcom's revenue guidance, which is nearly $2 billion ahead of analysts' expectations, also helps boost confidence, as well as its projection for $10.7 billion in AI revenue in the current quarter. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: Hammond Power Solutions Announces Fourth Quarter and Year End 2025 Financial Results Conference Call and Webcast Notification
data.description:
GUELPH, Ontario, March 04, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Hammond Power Solutions Inc. (“HPS” or the “Company”) today announced that it will release its financial results for the Fourth Quarter and Year ended December 31, 2025, on Thursday, March 19, 2026, after markets close. Following the release of its financial results, HPS will hold a conference call and webcast on Friday, March 20, 2026, at 9:00 a.m. Eastern Time.

This call can be accessed by registration through participant links:

Date/Time: March 20, 2026/ 9 a.m. ET

Live Call Participant Registration Link:

https://register-conf.media-server.com/register/BI56f91c9310bd4e8e9ace3f42be252017

Audio-Only Webcast:

https://edge.media-server.com/mmc/p/8vbmykvd

A webcast replay will be available for 12 months.

ABOUT HAMMOND POWER SOLUTIONS INC.

Hammond Power Solutions Inc. (“HPS” or the “Company”) enables electrification through its broad range of dry-type transformers, power quality products and related magnetics. HPS’ standard and custom-designed products are essential and ubiquitous in electrical distribution networks through an extensive range of end-user applications. The company has manufacturing plants in Canada, the United States (U.S.), Mexico and India and sells its products around the globe. HPS shares are listed on the Toronto Stock Exchange and trade under the symbol HPS.A

Hammond Power Solutions – Energizing Our World

For further information, please contact:

David Feick

Investor Relations (519) 822-2441

ir@hammondpowersolutions.com

data.title: Broadcom Sales Rise as AI Developers Build More Products and Agents — Update
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Broadcom posted higher sales in the latest quarter as the type and number of AI customers kept expanding.

AI revenue in the quarter more than doubled to $8.4 billion, driven by demand for custom AI accelerators and networking, Chief Executive Hock Tan. Demand is now coming from both hyperscalers and regular companies that are looking to build out their own AI agents, he told investors on a call Wednesday.

"Some of them are hyperscalers, some of them are not hyperscalers, but they all have one thing in common, which is to create LLMS, productize it and generate platforms," Tan said.

The semiconductor and software maker's customer base is expanding beyond its core focus of hyperscalers like Google and Anthropic, Tan said. Customers now want help building out AI agents, code assistance platforms and consumer subscription platforms like OpenAI's ChatGPT.

Broadcom expects OpenAI to deploy its first generation of XPUs in 2027 with more than one gigawatt of compute capacity, Tan said. That will make the company Broadcom's sixth major hyperscaler customer, Tan said.

The company posted a first-quarter profit of $7.35 billion, or $1.50 a share, compared with $5.50 billion, or $1.14 a share, a year earlier.

Stripping out certain one-time items, adjusted per-share earnings were $2.05, ahead of the $2.03 anticipated by analysts, according to FactSet.

Revenue rose 29% to $19.31 billion. Analysts surveyed by FactSet forecast revenue of $19.26 billion.

The company also said its board authorized a new share buyback program of up to $10 billion.

Broadcom's big hyperscaler customers are starting to shift from training AI to deploying it for users. Tan said that transition has happened faster than expected, and he anticipates it will drive more demand for Broadcom's offerings.

"These five, six customers of ours are on the path to creating their own custom accelerators," he said. "I think we're going to see demand keep picking up."

In the current quarter, Broadcom expects revenue to be $22 billion, above analysts' projection of $20.5 billion.

Tan told analysts that AI networking is projected to grow to 40% of total AI revenue as Broadcom gains more share in that market.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Peru's Pluspetrol halts gas production after pipeline leak
data.description:
Peru's Pluspetrol suspended liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) production following a pipeline rupture and leak in the country's Amazon region, the company said in a statement on Wednesday.

The rupture of the pipeline, operated by Transportadora de Gas del Peru (TGP), occurred Sunday in the district of Megantoni, in the Cusco region, a few kilometers from the Camisea gas field where Pluspetrol extracts natural gas.

The outage has cut off the flow of natural gas to Pluspetrol's fractionation plant in Pisco. The facility supplies approximately 70% of Peru's LPG consumption, according to company data.

TGP said on Monday it would implement temporary restrictions on gas supplies to industrial and electricity sector users while it repairs the leak.

Pluspetrol said its Pisco and Malvinas plants "remain ready to resume full production capacity once TGP has restarted its natural gas and gas liquids pipeline transportation service."

The company added that it is coordinating with national authorities and has made logistical resources available to TGP to resolve the situation.

Peru's Ministry of Energy and Mines also declared a 14-day emergency for natural gas transportation on Monday, a move intended to prioritize domestic market supply.

data.title: White House says Trump using preventative skin treatment for neck rash, offers no other details
data.description:
Key points:
  • Red neck rash seen in photographs on Monday
  • White House declines further details on Trump's skin condition
  • Trump's health scrutinized due to age and visible conditions

By Bo Erickson

U.S. President Donald Trump is using a preventative treatment for a red rash on his neck, according to his physician, but the White House declined on Wednesday to share further details about the condition.

A red, blotchy rash was visible on the right side of Trump's neck just above the collar line in photographs from his appearance at a Medal of Honor ceremony on Monday.

In a statement after the event, Dr. Sean Barbabella, the White House physician, said Trump was using a common cream as "a preventative skin treatment."

"The President is using this treatment for one week, and the redness is expected to last for a few weeks," Barbabella said.

Asked on Wednesday about why the treatment was necessary, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters she had no additional information to share beyond the details provided in the physician's statement.

Trump's health, at age 79, has been more of a focus in his second term, as bruises on his hands and swelling in his legs have been visible at times.

In January, Trump attributed the hand bruising to his frequent use of aspirin, and the White House said the president hit his hand on a table when traveling abroad.

Last July, after the president's ankles appeared swollen, the White House physician said an ultrasound on the president's legs "revealed chronic venous insufficiency, a benign and common condition, particularly in individuals over the age of 70."

As he took office for a second term last year, Trump was the oldest U.S. president ever inaugurated, and he frequently compares his health to former Democratic President Joe Biden.

data.title: Everspin Technologies Inc reports results for the quarter ended December 31 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• Everspin Technologies Inc reported quarterly adjusted earnings of 11 cents​​ per share for the quarter ended December 31, lower than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of 12 cents. The mean expectation of two analysts for the quarter was for earnings of 12 cents per share. Wall Street expected results to range from 11 cents to 12 cents per share.

  • Revenue rose 11.8% to $14.80 million from a year ago; analysts expected $14.45 million.

  • Everspin Technologies Inc's reported EPS for the quarter was 5 cents​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $1.2 million.

  • Everspin Technologies Inc shares had risen by 14.3% this quarter.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts was unchanged in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, there have been no earnings estimate revisions by analysts covering the company.

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 2 "strong buy" or "buy," no "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the semiconductors peer group is also "buy"

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Everspin Technologies Inc is $12.75, about 15.4% above its last closing price of $10.79

data.title: Extent of Endeavour's Turnaround Remains Unclear — Market Talk
data.description: The extent of Endeavour Group's retail turnaround remains uncertain, UBS analysts warn. Keeping a neutral rating on the Australian alcohol and hospitality retailer, they tell clients in a note that recent promotion-led retail sales growth may prove difficult to sustain over the medium term due to structural challenges. They think that Endeavour either needs to reinvest cost savings into further pricing initiatives or offsetting operating deleverage. This would moderate the extent of the turnaround, they add. UBS lifts its target price 6.7% to A$4.00, supported by the strength of its hotels business. Shares are up 3.1% at A$3.96. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Trump administration asks judge to dismiss ABA lawsuit over law firm orders
data.description:
By Mike Scarcella

The U.S. Justice Department urged a federal judge on Wednesday to dismiss a lawsuit by the American Bar Association that seeks to bar President Donald Trump from targeting major law firms over their affiliations with his perceived political opponents.

The administration argued at a hearing that the ABA had not shown it has standing to sue, saying it had no policy focused on law firms and that any alleged threat to ABA members from the White House was speculative.

U.S. District Judge Amir Ali in Washington appeared skeptical of those arguments, pointing to public statements from the White House and five executive orders Trump issued early last year seeking to punish major firms. He also cited settlements that nine firms reached with the administration to rescind or avoid similar actions against them.

"Why is that not enough?" Ali asked a lawyer for the government.

The ABA, a voluntary lawyer membership organization that advocates for the legal profession, sued the administration in June to stop the White House from using executive orders to punish law firms that Trump has accused of bias and of "weaponizing" the legal system against him and his allies.

Justice Department lawyer Abhishek Kambli on Wednesday argued that the ABA and its members faced no imminent threat, and that the lawsuit should be dismissed. Arguing for the ABA, attorney Stephen Shackelford said the group had plausibly shown at an early stage in the case that the Trump administration had formed and acted on a policy to intimidate law firms.

Trump in March and April last year issued orders against five firms that sought to restrict their lawyers' access to federal buildings and to end government contracts held by their clients. One of the firms quickly settled with the administration, and eight other firms preemptively struck deals with the White House to avoid similar orders.

Four of the firms hit with Trump's executive orders — Perkins Coie; Jenner & Block; Wilmer Cutler Pickering Hale and Dorr; and Susman Godfrey — sued the administration in response and won rulings permanently blocking the orders against them as unconstitutional.

The administration dropped its appeals of those rulings on Monday before abruptly reversing course the following day, saying it intended to move ahead with the cases.

Ali asked Kambli whether the government's about-face in the appeals was relevant to his consideration of the ABA's lawsuit. Kambli said it was not, but did not elaborate.

Kambli, who is handling those appeals, on Wednesday declined to comment about what circumstances led to the reversal.

The ABA's lawsuit said Trump’s crackdown on law firms he disfavors made it harder for some public-interest organizations to find lawyers for new matters "because counsel who previously were willing to take on such work are no longer willing to do so."

Reuters in a special report published in July showed how some firms were retreating from public interest legal work in the wake of Trump's pressure campaign.

The case is American Bar Association v. Executive Office of the U.S. President, U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia, No. 1:25-cv-01888.

For American Bar Association: Stephen Shackelford and Jillian Hewitt of Susman Godfrey

For the president: Abhishek Kambli and James Wen of the Justice Department

Read more:

How Trump’s crackdown on law firms is undermining legal defenses for the vulnerable

What Republican, Democratic judges said about Trump’s law firm orders

American Bar Association sues to block Trump's attacks on law firms

Law firms’ deals with Trump roil their staff, deepen industry rifts

Why target these law firms? For Trump, it's personal

data.title: SES: 2025 revenue soared nearly tenfold to $21M; 2026 outlook targets 43–67% growth, led by ESS
data.description:
Full year 2025 revenue surged to $21M, driven by ESS, drones, and materials, with strong cost discipline and a $200M liquidity position. 2026 guidance targets $30–$35M revenue, led by ESS, as drone and materials ramp in H2. One-time OEM service revenue will not recur.

data.title: CBRL: Q2 revenue fell 7.9% year-over-year, but guest metrics and cost savings showed improvement
data.description:
Q2 revenue declined 7.9% year-over-year to $874.8 million, with adjusted EBITDA at $38.2 million. Guest experience metrics improved, and FY26 guidance anticipates $3.24–$3.27 billion in revenue and $85–$100 million in adjusted EBITDA.

data.title: LNR: Record earnings and strong mobility growth offset industrial softness, with robust outlook for 2026
data.description:
Record 2025 earnings driven by mobility growth and strategic acquisitions offset industrial softness. Strong cash flow, low leverage, and disciplined capital allocation support continued investment and shareholder returns, with 2026 guidance unchanged and mobility expected to lead growth.
